OpenAI's spending cuts trigger a chain reaction: Global memory chip prices begin to fall. It was OpenAI's explosive growth that had previously triggered the surge in memory prices. As an indispensable core component in consumer electronics and data centers, suppliers' production capacity simply could not meet the massive demand from the AI ​​industry. Data from market research firm TrendForce shows that memory prices have skyrocketed by about seven times in the past year. However, as OpenAI begins to put the brakes on its massive data center investments, this price tsunami is finally showing signs of abating. For example, some DDR5 memory kits have seen price reductions of up to $100. According to follow-up reports from industry media, a 32GB memory module on Amazon... The price of the capacity kit has fallen sharply from its recent high of $490 to $370. However, as the creator behind ChatGPT, OpenAI has recently halted several extremely expensive star projects, leading the market to question whether they can still absorb so many chips. Just last week, in order to control ever-increasing operating costs, they shut down the once-promising AI video generation application Sora. Earlier this month, they also tore up a multi-billion dollar partnership with Oracle and abandoned plans to expand their Texas super data center.

... `/v1/agent/completions` supports executing each request as a complete Agent. New endpoint features include: full drop-in compatibility with the existing OpenAI SDK; a unified entry point with built-in routing, token tracking, billing, and logging; and support for over 1000 models including OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini. data.title: Polymarket Smart Money Copy Trading Guide: From Address Selection to Avoiding Pitfalls
data.description:
Opening the Polymarket leaderboard, your first thought might be: This address made $200,000, is betting with them a sure thing? Not necessarily.

Behind such huge profits might be 50 consecutive successful bets in various markets, or it might be a single, large bet that just happened to be right. The former is a systematic profit model worth replicating, while the latter is unsustainable survivorship bias.

This article will guide you through two core questions:

  • How to penetrate data noise and identify real "smart money"?

  • After finding the address, how do you implement it using different strategies?

I. Finding Smart Money: The Essence of Prediction Markets is Information Game

Prediction markets are fundamentally different from secondary market cryptocurrency trading: they are extremely segmented information games.

Each market target represents a specific professional problem:

  • Will a movie break 100 million yuan in its opening week box office? (Theatrical release schedule, pre-sale data)

  • Will the highest temperature in a certain city exceed 35 degrees Celsius tomorrow? (Weather model, historical average)

  • Will Iran launch an attack on Israel this month? (Geopolitics, intelligence monitoring)

These areas have extremely high information barriers.

Copying smart money essentially involves finding people with a higher level of knowledge than you in a specific vertical field. So where do you find these people? Biteye's previous article, "Mastering Polymarket: 7 Tools Are Enough," introduced 7 Polymarket tools. Taking Polymarket Analytics as an example, we must be wary of the following three pitfalls when selecting tools: 1. False Profit/Loss (PNL) Data: Polymarket's data structure is extremely complex, involving various on-chain operations such as buying, splitting, merging, and redeeming. Many tools (even the official website) can have significantly inaccurate PNL figures if the wrong calculation dimension is selected. True smart money should calculate based on event dimensions, comprehensively considering inflows, outflows, and current holding market value. 2. Interference from Arbitrage Bots: For example, automatedAltradingbot, which often appears on lists. These addresses profit through cross-market arbitrage or providing liquidity. While their win rate is impressive, each of their trades has a hedging position. If you only follow one of their strategies, the risk will be completely asymmetrical. 3. High win rate does not equal high expectations. Some addresses specifically target markets with a win rate of over 98% that are about to settle, aiming to profit from the final $0.02 spread. Although this strategy has a near 100% win rate, it offers no profit margin for copy traders and may even result in losses due to transaction fees.

II. Four Dimensions for Screening Smart Money

After finding the ranking list, the next step is screening. Smart money has two bottom-line conditions:

  • Profits must conform to the logic of the Kelly Criterion

  • At the same time, there cannot be too large a single loss.

The core idea of ​​the Kelly Criterion is: the betting size must match the win rate and odds; you can't go all in just because you feel confident in a particular trade.

Traders who truly understand risk management calculate every position, preventing a single loss from wiping out all previous profits. Therefore, before examining specific data, exclude two types of addresses: those with abnormally large total losses and those with a history of massive single losses. Even if the total PNL of such addresses is positive, their risk management logic is flawed. For the remaining addresses, use four dimensions to evaluate them: 1. Win Rate. Win rate is a core indicator for determining whether an address is consistently profitable, but it must be considered in conjunction with PNL. High win rate but low PNL: This often indicates late-day trading, where even if you win, the profit is minimal; Low win rate but high PNL: This might mean a few heavily leveraged bets were successful by chance. 2. Number of Markets: A small sample size renders the win rate meaningless. Winning five consecutive coin tosses isn't a low probability, but nobody considers it significant. A 70% win rate on 300 markets is far more valuable than an 80% win rate on 10 markets. The more markets involved, the harder it is to explain profits as luck. However, too many markets might indicate the address belongs to a bot, making copy trading unnecessary. 3. Holding Period Addresses with short holding periods enter and exit within hours. By the time you notice their buy, the news may already be reflected in the price, and following them is just chasing the high, or even becoming a liquidity drain. Addresses with long holding periods are more like those that have pre-emptively predicted a move. When you notice them, there's often enough time to follow, making this the most friendly type for ordinary copy traders. 4. Profit Structure: Diversified Profits or Relying on One Big Bet A good overall PNL doesn't mean you're profitable in every market. Some addresses are propped up by one or two correct large bets, while the rest are mostly losing money. This structure is difficult to replicate—you don't know where they'll bet next, or whether it's a prediction or luck. Stable smart money profits should be spread across multiple markets, not concentrated in a few exceptionally large trades. What does a healthy address look like? Take BeefSlayer, a weather leaderboard, as an example. At a glance, the data shows participation in 1,360 independent markets, a total of 2,500 trades, a net profit of $41,367, a win rate of 61.2%, and an average bet of $196 per trade. The scatter plot on the right shows that profits are distributed across various win rate ranges, not concentrated in a few exceptionally large trades. Large positions are concentrated in the 60%-90% win rate range, consistent with the Kelly Criterion: the more confident the market, the larger the bet; for uncertain markets, control position size.

Money Management: On average, each bet is only $196. In 2,500 trades, there has never been a single instance of heavily leveraged betting that wiped out the account, demonstrating stable risk control.

III. Copy Trading Practice: Automated Tools vs. Subjective Judgment

After finding a worthwhile address to copy, how exactly do you operate it? There are roughly two paths: one is to directly use a robot to automatically copy trades, which is convenient but has limitations; the other is to use the smart money's position as a reference signal, and then make your own judgment before deciding whether to copy. Each method has its applicable scenarios, which will be discussed below.

Strategy 1: Copy Trading Bots Currently, there are readily available copy trading bots on the market. Simply set the target address, and they will automatically copy trades. Common ones include: Polygun @Polygun_: A Telegram copy trading bot that recently acquired Polymarket Analytics. The latter is a data analytics platform; the merger allows for both analysis and direct copy trading execution. Kreo @kreoapp: A Telegram bot that monitors on-chain smart money activity in real time and automatically copies trades. It supports setting daily loss limits and stop-loss rules and covers both Polymarket and Kalshi platforms. PolyHub (Hubble) @MeetPolyHub: A tool under Hubble that helps users identify smart money addresses on Polymarket. It has now launched a copy trading tool. However, copy trading bots are not as simple as they seem. After trying copy trading, I found three problems: Problem 1: Inequal Capital Size. Suppose you are following an address with $100,000. They bet $500 in a market, which is 0.5% of their total capital. If you only have $100 in your copy trading wallet, and you also copy proportionally, you would only spend $0.50. However, Polymarket's minimum transaction amount is $1, so copy trading is prone to failure. **Question 2: Market Liquidity Constraints.** Smart money has large sums of money; a single purchase by them can consume a large portion of the order book's liquidity. When a copy order is triggered, there may not be enough remaining order book space. You either won't be able to buy at the same price, or you won't be able to buy a sufficient quantity. **Question 3: Pending Order Execution Mechanism.** Most Polymarket traders rarely use market orders; the vast majority place buy orders as takers at their desired prices. Pending orders tend to result in small orders being executed consecutively. For copy trading, choosing the right copy trading method becomes difficult: If you choose to copy based on portfolio proportions, small pending orders can easily prevent your own orders from reaching the minimum trade amount of $1; if you choose a fixed amount per trade, multiple consecutive trades with the same option can easily occur, leading to a significant position shift. If the trader subsequently chooses to hedge, there may not be enough funds to buy into the hedging position. These two problems are not obvious when the capital is small, but they become increasingly serious as the copy trading capital increases. **Strategy Two: Subjective Copy Trading** Treat smart money positions as signals, make your own judgments, and then decide whether to follow. **Step One: Monitoring Target Addresses** After finding an address worth following, don't rely on manual refreshing to discover its new activity. The actual operation involves using tools to monitor its on-chain transactions. Once it makes a new buy or sell, you'll receive a notification immediately. You can use Kreo's Tg monitoring bot; when the monitored wallet address makes a transaction, you'll receive a notification immediately. Step Two: Determining Why the Wallet Bought. After receiving the transaction record notification, we need to determine why the address made the purchase. Look at the timing of their entry: Was it right after a major news story? If it's a news-driven trade, by the time you receive the notification and follow up, the market price may have already priced in the news, and you'll be chasing the high. If they preemptively entered the market before the news broke, it means they're trading based on their own judgment. This signal is more valuable, and you still have time to follow. If their entry price is close to 100, it means the address is engaging in "end-of-day trading," the market outcome is basically certain, and they're capturing the last bit of profit. Whether to follow this position depends on whether the remaining potential is worthwhile for you. Step 3: Determining if it's worth following. After determining the wallet's strategy, there are two more things to check before copying: Price Spread: The larger the price spread, the higher your entry cost is compared to theirs, compressing potential profits and amplifying risks. Position Size: The higher the proportion of this position to their total capital, the more confident they are, and the more worthy the signal is. Fourth, Avoiding Pitfalls: Why do I still lose money copying? Copying sounds simple, but in practice, many details can lead to losses. Here are three common mistakes I've made. Myth 1: The bot's position sizing mode is incorrect. Bots typically have four position sizing modes: fixed amount, exact copy, proportional, and portfolio weight. Initially, I used proportional copy, but the other party's capital was 100 times mine. If they bought 1%, I would only buy 1%, resulting in an absolute amount of only a few cents, which was impossible to buy into. Smart money betting on low-probability markets relies on small positions to potentially gain large returns, and I couldn't keep up with that profit. So I switched to fixed amount copy, buying a fixed $1 each time. But then another problem arose: low-probability markets are inherently more likely to result in losses. Smart money might win once out of 100 trades to break even, but by following their trades several times a day, the money would quickly be wiped out, and I would never see that big win. The conclusion is: Before using a bot to copy trades, you must first understand its profit structure. If its profits mainly come from low-probability markets, it will be difficult for the bot to replicate its strategy. Misconception Two: Following Too Late Notices It's impossible for a person to stare at the screen all the time. By the time they see the monitoring push and check the market, the price has often already risen from $0.35 to $0.72. Following at this point means your cost is twice as high, and the remaining profit potential is less than $0.28. However, if the judgment is wrong, there is still a downside potential of $0.72, making the risk-reward ratio completely unbalanced. Myth 3: You're Holding On While Smart Money Clears Out. You monitor smart money starting to reduce their positions, thinking, "The direction is still right, let's wait and see," only to find they've cleared out their entire position while you're still fully invested. The underlying logic of copy trading is borrowing their information and judgment. When they're exiting at a loss, you still hold onto hope—this is why I lose the most money in copy trading. This is similar to doing LP (Limited Partner) trading on Polymarket: when your order is accidentally filled, and the spread is large, you're usually reluctant to cut your losses, always thinking the price will rebound, only to see it continue to fall. Behind these three myths lies a common problem: copy trading easily leads to abandoning independent thinking. Robots execute for you, smart money makes judgments for you; it seems like you don't need to do anything, but once a problem arises, you have no idea where you went wrong or how to adjust. V. In Conclusion: Copy trading is the beginning, understanding is the next level. Copy trading is a crutch; it can help you navigate the deeper waters of market prediction, but it can't replace your walking. The real value isn't what smart money "bought," but "why they bought." When you can deduce the logic behind their trades, copy trading is no longer your lifeline, but a filtering tool to improve efficiency. This is the necessary path to evolve from a "newbie" to "smart money."

data.title: The focus of AI payment is not the payment itself.
data.description: "Please reorder the milk tea I bought from that shop yesterday." If one day you could actually say this to your phone, and it would find the merchant, confirm the product, place the order, pay, and arrange delivery, you probably wouldn't first think of a payment revolution. You'd likely just feel that your phone is finally starting to act as a capable assistant. This might be a more appropriate starting point for our discussion of AI payments today. Most people's understanding of AI payments isn't about giving AI a wallet, nor is it about letting a robot decide how to spend money. What people truly experience is whether a single sentence can directly become a transaction. You state your needs, and the system handles the rest. Therefore, what's truly worth discussing about AI payments isn't often the word "payment," as that word is too abstract and distant. For most internet users, the general feeling is: an app or AI assistant can handle their shopping needs directly when they express them in plain language. This is the intuition of Chinese users. If we only look at the Chinese market, AI payment isn't actually that unfamiliar. The reason is simple. Chinese users already live in a very mature mobile internet ecosystem: food delivery, e-commerce, maps, ride-hailing, local services, mobile payment, and instant delivery are all interconnected. Often, what users want isn't a completely new payment method, but rather to avoid having to switch between apps and repeatedly click the same buttons. In China, AI payment is more like an upgrade to the e-commerce shopping experience than a complete reconstruction of the payment system from scratch. Alibaba's upgrade of the Qwen App in January this year is a typical example of this direction. According to the official introduction, Qwen has integrated with services within the Alibaba ecosystem, including Taobao, Taobao Flash Sale, Alipay, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. Users can order food, complete payments in chat, and plan and book trips with just a sentence. It seems like AI has become smarter, but more accurately, it's the platform's existing capabilities that have been reorganized. This is why AI payment is most easily implemented in China not in grand visions, but in very everyday scenarios. For example, "Buy another box of the printer paper I bought last week and deliver it to my office." Or, "Order another delivery from that restaurant yesterday and have it delivered to my home." Or, "Book a restaurant close to the office, for four people, with a budget under 500 yuan." Users understand these commands immediately. Because the AI ​​here isn't some mysterious new financial tool; it's simply a smarter shopping assistant. What the people need, the endlessly competitive Chinese internet giants will do. Today, there are roughly three paths to AI-powered payments. One is to let AI watch the screen and operate on your behalf, clicking buttons, filling in information, and submitting the process like a human. The advantage of this approach is that it can be tested anywhere, but the disadvantages are also obvious: problems easily arise when the page changes, and you often still need to confirm the key parts yourself. For example, the Doubao phone, which was immediately attacked by WeChat upon its release. Another approach is for the platform to simply hand over its service capabilities directly to AI. For Chinese giants, this path is actually the smoothest. Because payment, merchants, maps, orders, and delivery are already under their control. AI doesn't need to imitate the user clicking around; it can directly access the capabilities within the system. In terms of user experience, it's "I said one thing, and it actually did it for me." For example, Alibaba's Qianwen and ByteDance's Doubao. The third approach is even more difficult, involving cross-platform, cross-website, and cross-company collaborations. At this stage, the issue isn't just about product usability, but whether others are willing to let your AI represent users in this process. Currently, we haven't seen any Chinese internet companies attempting this hard-line approach. Today, China's greatest advantage clearly lies in the first two approaches, especially the second. This is why, for Chinese users, AI payment may not seem groundbreaking; their overall feeling is likely not "AI payment has arrived," but rather "mobile shopping is more convenient." The situation is different overseas. However, once we shift our perspective overseas, things become somewhat different. While overseas markets also have e-commerce, food delivery, maps, and payments, they lack the highly integrated platform structure found in China. Service entry points, merchant systems, payment tools, and fulfillment networks are often controlled by different companies. Therefore, when AI wants to complete a transaction on behalf of a user, it faces not just the question of "can the order be placed?", but also "will others accept you as an AI agent?" This is why overseas AI payments have suddenly become complex. A typical example is the conflict between Amazon and Perplexity. Perplexity's Comet browser touts AI shopping as a key selling point, stating in its official description that it can help users compare products, read reviews, and go all the way to checkout. This aligns with many people's most intuitive imagination of AI shopping: instead of browsing websites one by one, AI will pick and buy for you. But the problem lies precisely here. According to court documents dated March 9, 2026, a core argument Amazon made in its lawsuit was that Perplexity's AI agent accessed user accounts and attempted to perform actions "with the user's consent, but without Amazon's consent." Amazon's reasons for disagreeing are largely the same as WeChat's reasons for disagreeing with Doubao Mobile: authorizing AI doesn't mean the website also authorizes it. Your willingness to let it buy things for you doesn't mean the platform is willing to allow a third-party agent into its system to initiate transactions on your behalf. While the stated reason is for customer privacy and security, the core concern is that AI shopping will directly divert billions of dollars of advertising revenue from its sellers. After all, AI shopping is straightforward; it goes straight for the product and doesn't care about ads. In China, these kinds of conflicts are often resolved by the platform's integrated capabilities; however, overseas, once AI needs to complete transactions across websites, merchants, and payment networks, these issues become unavoidable. Therefore, overseas, the primary question for AI payments isn't "how easy it is to use," but rather "whether this can be completed in a closed-loop end-to-end manner." This is why Google, Stripe, and Coinbase have recently been focusing their efforts on this area. They all see the same problem: in the past, internet transactions assumed that the order was placed by a person, who was sitting in front of the screen, browsing, confirming, and paying. But once the order is placed by an authorized AI, that assumption no longer holds true. How does the website know that this AI was truly sent by them? How can the merchant confirm that it hasn't made any unauthorized purchases? How should payment institutions determine whether or not to release the funds? Google aims to address the fundamental trust issue first. In September 2025, it launched AP2, not to teach AI how to make payments, but to answer the fundamental question: when AI initiates a payment on behalf of a user, what makes the system believe it's genuine? Google's approach is to add a verifiable "authorization certificate" to these transactions. AP2 includes two key components: a Cart Mandate, which can be understood as the user's signature confirming the specific purchase; and a Payment Mandate, shown to the payment network and card issuer, informing them that the transaction was initiated by an agent, whether the user was present, and the transaction's context. Google later integrated this system with PayPal, creating a more complete merchant solution: merchants can use their own conversational shopping assistant to serve users, and at the payment stage, PayPal Agent takes over, using AP2 to complete the authorization and payment process. Ultimately, Google's approach addresses the fundamental question of "why trust AI to place orders." Stripe's approach is closer to merchants and platforms. It actually involves two layers. The first layer is the ACP, or Agentic Commerce Protocol, launched in September 2025 with OpenAI. You can think of it as a "standard for AI-readable checkout." Stripe doesn't want AI to mimic humans clicking web pages and filling out forms; instead, it wants merchants to proactively open up their capabilities regarding products, inventory, and checkout, allowing AI to initiate purchases using a standardized interface. The advantage of this approach is that merchants remain primarily responsible for orders and fulfillment; product display, order processing, and risk control assessments are still under their control, but they don't need to rebuild their systems for each AI platform individually. Stripe later launched the Agentic Commerce Suite, making catalog access, checkout, and payment a one-stop tool to lower the barrier to entry for merchants. However, Stripe also saw a completely different scenario. It's not "AI helping people buy things," but rather "software paying for software directly." Therefore, in March 2026, it launched Machine Payments. In this scenario, AI doesn't buy a Coke, but rather APIs, data, computing power, content access rights, or even pay-per-use services. Stripe's solution is that merchants can directly convert their interfaces to pay-per-use rates, as low as 0.01 USDC, with the money going into Stripe's account, and final settlement and reconciliation still done in Stripe's familiar way. For agents, there's no need to register an account, apply for an API key, or go through a bunch of manual processes; they can make payments while making inquiries. In other words, Stripe is building both an AI shopping checkout system and a micro-payment track between machines. Coinbase takes a different approach. It's more like assuming that in the future, many AIs won't be "shopping assistants" but rather "software with wallets." Therefore, in February 2026, it launched Agentic Wallets. The core of this isn't just making AI better at browsing websites, but giving AI a real wallet that can spend, receive, and transact, and that this wallet has security boundaries. Coinbase's system includes the wallet infrastructure itself, the x402 machine payment protocol, and pre-packaged capabilities such as deposits, transfers, transactions, and earnings management. Its main focus is on solving a different type of problem: if an AI wants to buy computing power, data, pay API fees, or complete some kind of automated transaction on-chain, can it avoid waiting for confirmation every time? Coinbase's official documentation calls this agent an "independent economic agent." This term is a bit strong, but the meaning is clear: what Coinbase wants to do is not to make AI more like a shopping assistant, but to make AI itself a software entity that can handle income, expenditure, settlement, and transactions. If you look at these three paths together, the differences become clear. Google wants to first solve "how do you prove that this transaction was truly authorized by me?" Stripe is more concerned with "how merchants can connect their products and checkout capabilities to AI." Coinbase, on the other hand, has taken a step further to solve "if the payer is software, how does it complete income and expenditure on its own?" This is precisely why, while they all appear to be working on AI payments, their focuses differ. Google is more like adding a layer of trust to cross-platform proxy transactions; Stripe aims to integrate AI shopping and AI-driven service calls into existing business networks; Coinbase, on the other hand, is betting on a world where software directly pays for software. Two Stories At this point, the difference becomes clear. In China, AI payments are more like a natural extension of a mature internet system. Platforms have already organized merchants, payments, delivery, maps, and local services; AI's role is to rearrange these capabilities, allowing users to go from "operating themselves" to "simply speaking." Overseas, AI payments are more like a belated catch-up. Because no platform naturally controls the entire transaction chain, once AI is to actually complete transactions for users, it immediately encounters real-world problems such as whether websites will allow it, whether merchants will accept it, whether payment institutions will recognize it, and who will be responsible if problems arise. In China, it's more like pushing the online shopping experience a step further. Overseas, it's more like renegotiating the rules for participation by multiple parties in internet software and online consumption scenarios. This is why Chinese users' first reaction to AI payments might be, "This function is quite convenient"; while overseas markets' perception of AI payments is more like, "The original way of online transactions may need to be changed." Returning to the initial scenario... "Please place another order for the milk tea I bought yesterday and deliver it to my door." The truly interesting part of this sentence isn't the word "payment," but rather how it delegates many tasks that you would normally have to do manually to the system. It needs to know which store you mentioned, which item, its specifications, delivery location, and payment method. Payment is important, of course, but it's just the least significant step in the whole process. Therefore, the focus of AI payment isn't the payment itself. What people really care about isn't which protocol the backend uses, whether it's on-chain settlement, whether it uses stablecoins, or whether it uses encryption algorithms. Ultimately, people will use a very simple standard to judge its usefulness: I told the AI ​​something, can it actually get it done for me?

data.title: Why did the war not only drive up oil prices, but also cause Circle's stock price to soar?
data.description:
Foreword

There is a type of company that actually profits when global situations deteriorate. Defense contractors, oil giants, gold mining companies. These are obvious examples; their business models are built on instability and incorporate that instability into their prices.

Circle shouldn't belong to this category. Its token value has always been fixed at $1, by design. Stability is at the heart of its product. However, Circle's stock price has surged from $49.90 on February 5th to approximately $123 today, more than doubling in just five weeks. Meanwhile, the entire cryptocurrency market is still 44% lower than its October peak.

Due to the increasingly volatile global situation, a company whose product is designed to maintain price stability has become one of the most sought-after trading targets in the market.

I want to explain how it works, why it's more interesting than it seems, and what it shows us about the essence of Circle and how it differentiates it from other products currently available for purchase. What is Circle (we'll talk about that later, of course)? Leaving aside branding, payment concepts, and infrastructure, you'll find that Circle's essence is: it holds U.S. Treasury bonds. Every dollar of USDC in circulation is backed by one dollar of short-term government bonds. The interest on these bonds goes to Circle. This accounts for approximately 90% of the company's quarterly revenue. Its business model is actually quite simple: Circle is a money market fund that issues stablecoins. This means that Circle's revenue depends on only one key factor: the federal funds rate. When interest rates are high, Treasury yields are high, and Circle earns more for every USDC it issues. When interest rates are low, revenue decreases. Everything else is secondary. The following is a series of events that led to a 150% rebound in stock prices from their February lows. Since February 28th, the Iranian conflict has driven oil prices up by approximately 35%. Oil prices exceeding $100 indicate inflation concerns, which mean that any rate cut by the Federal Reserve would be seen as reckless. Maintaining interest rates unchanged on March 18th was practically a foregone conclusion. Even before the war broke out, the CME FedWatch tool indicated a greater than 90% probability of unchanged rates. The war's real impact will be on the market landscape for the entire year. Before the conflict, the market expected two interest rate cuts of 25 basis points each in 2026. After the conflict broke out, the number of rate cuts dropped to one, at the earliest after September. The probability of no rate cuts at all in 2026 almost doubled. With interest rates expected to remain high for an extended period, Circle's Treasury reserve yield continued to rise. Higher yields mean more revenue. More revenue means a higher stock price. War broke out, and a stablecoin issuer benefited. This was completely unexpected. As background, the pessimistic expectation that Circle's stock price would fall to $49 in February was essentially a bet on interest rate cuts. The market expected the Federal Reserve to cut rates multiple times in 2026, which would directly compress Circle's reserve income. A rough estimate: based on the current USDC supply of $79 billion, each 25 basis point rate cut would reduce Circle's annualized revenue by $40 million to $60 million. Two interest rate cuts would have reduced its revenue by nearly $100 million by the end of the year. However, the war changed that expectation overnight. This wasn't because Circle itself changed, but because the macroeconomic context that was thought to weaken this argument no longer applied.

How the Squeeze Began

While the interest rate story kept the stock price high, the initial surge stemmed from position building.

Before the release of its fourth-quarter earnings report on February 25, approximately 17.8% of Circle's outstanding shares were shorted. Hedge funds had established a large number of short positions. Their logic was that interest rates would eventually fall, reserve income would decrease, and the company had no minimum income guarantee independent of interest rates. From a fundamental perspective, this argument seemed to make sense. Subsequently, Circle reported earnings per share of $0.43, higher than the market consensus of $0.16. Revenue was $770 million, higher than the expected $749 million.

On-chain USDC trading volume approached $12 trillion this quarter, a 247% year-over-year increase. Short covering occurred. The stock price surged 35% in a single trading day. 10x Research estimates that hedge funds lost approximately $500 million in a single day due to short positions. Subsequently, the short-selling war intensified, continuing the positive impact of the earnings report. Coinbase Issues Here's the part that didn't enter the upward narrative. Circle reported a net loss of $70 million in 2025, rather than a profit. The fourth quarter was strong, but the full year was weak. To understand why, you need to understand the Coinbase protocol, which is the most important yet most easily overlooked key to Circle's business. When USDC was initially launched in 2018, Circle and Coinbase formed a joint consortium to manage it. This consortium dissolved in 2023, giving Circle full control over the issuance of USDC. However, Coinbase retained a share of the profits. Coinbase takes 100% of the revenue from USDC reserves held on its platform and splits the rest 50/50 with Circle. In 2024, this arrangement directly gave Coinbase $908 million of Circle's total distribution costs of $1.01 billion. For roughly every dollar earned, 54 cents went to a company that neither issued tokens nor handled reserves. By early 2025, Coinbase held 22% of the total USDC supply, up from 5% in 2022. The more USDC grows on the Coinbase platform, the more Circle earns. The agreement automatically renews every three years, and Circle cannot unilaterally withdraw. The outcome of the next renegotiation will directly impact Circle's profit margin. In the fourth quarter of 2025, distribution costs alone reached $461 million, a 52% year-over-year increase. The full-year net loss of $70 million was partly due to a one-time stock-based compensation expense of $424 million following the IPO, making the book loss worse than the actual business situation. However, Circle's core business still faces structural cost issues that no interest rate environment can completely resolve. The market is pricing Circle as infrastructure. However, its profit and loss statement shows it as an interest rate trading company with high distribution costs. Both perspectives can hold true simultaneously, just priced differently. Currently, the market is paying for the best of both worlds. What makes this more than just a macro trade? USDC supply recently reached a record high of $79 billion, while the overall cryptocurrency market has fallen 44% from its October peak. This divergence warrants attention. Speculative assets typically fall when the market declines. The reason USDC continues to grow is that people use it to move funds, not to hold it as a speculative instrument. Demand for USDC surged in the Middle East during the Iranian conflict precisely because traditional banking systems became unreliable. When normal payment channels are disrupted, people use USDC for remittances and cross-border transfers. This is how payment infrastructure performs under pressure: usage increases, not decreases.

Transaction data also confirms this. In February alone, USDC's adjusted trading volume reached approximately $1.26 trillion, while USDT's trading volume was $514 billion during the same period. Although Tether's market capitalization is still as high as $184 billion, while USDC's market capitalization is only $79 billion, the difference in total supply is enormous. However, USDC's trading volume has now surpassed USDT's.

Dormant supply and active settlement are two different concepts. The former refers to where people store their funds, while the latter refers to the funds people use when they need to transfer value.

... Druckenmiller offered some insightful observations this week. In an interview with Morgan Stanley recorded on January 30 and previously released, he stated that he expects the global payment system to operate on stablecoins within the next 10 to 15 years, calling cryptocurrencies "a solution to a problem." This most authoritative macro investor today succinctly divides the cryptocurrency space in two: stablecoins are the inevitable infrastructure, while everything else is still searching for its reason for existence. This argument forms the theoretical basis for a bullish view on cryptocurrencies. Tokenized assets have grown from approximately $1.5 billion at the beginning of 2023 to approximately $26.5 billion today. Many of these products, including BlackRock's tokenized Treasury bond fund BUIDL (currently holding over $2 billion in assets), rely on USDC for subscription, redemption, and settlement processing. Prediction markets processed over $22 billion in transactions in 2025, primarily settled in USDC. Polymarket alone accounted for this. Visa now supports over 130 stablecoin-linked cards across 50 countries, with an annualized settlement volume of approximately $4.6 billion. The size of tokenized assets has grown from approximately $1.5 billion at the beginning of 2023 to approximately $26.5 billion today. Many such products, including BlackRock's tokenized treasury fund BUIDL (currently with over $2 billion in assets), rely on USDC for creation, redemption, and settlement. Prediction markets processed over $22 billion in transactions in 2025, the majority of which were settled in USDC. Polymarket alone achieved this. Visa now supports over 130 stablecoin-linked cards in 50 countries, with an annualized settlement volume of approximately $4.6 billion. Circle is also building the infrastructure beneath all of this. The Circle Payments Network connects 55 financial institutions, handling $5.7 billion in annual transactions, enabling banks and payment service providers to transfer USDC across borders and directly convert it into local currency. Circle's proprietary Layer-1 blockchain, Arc, is designed to fully support the institutional layer. Its settlement infrastructure does not rely on Ethereum or Solana. While Ethereum and Solana are currently too small to impact revenue, they are strategic investments for the future, addressing the possibility of declining interest rates. The AI ​​layer, though smaller in scale, is structurally significant. Data released in March by Circle's global marketing director showed that AI agents completed 140 million payments totaling $43 million in the past nine months. 98.6% of these transactions were settled in USDC, with an average transaction value of $0.31. Currently, over 400,000 AI agents have purchasing power. While the amount is still small, the direction of development is undeniable. If AI agents need to pay each other for computation, data access, and API calls at extremely high frequency and in extremely low amounts (less than $0.25), they need a payment method that can settle instantly and at zero cost. Circle launched Nanopayments for this purpose. Nanopayments offers USDC transfers with no gas fees as low as $0.000001, with transactions packaged off-chain and settled in batches. The testnet currently supports 12 blockchains, including Arbitrum, Base, and Ethereum. This is the market's current price of $123 per share for Circle. The company is at the heart of tokenized finance, AI agent commerce, cross-border payments, and prediction markets, and benefits from the regulatory advantages of the GENIUS Act and the CLARITY Act, which may pass before summer. Bernstein has a target price of $190, Clear Street has a target price of $136, while Seaport Global, the most bullish on Circle on Wall Street, has a target price of $280. The Lingering Tension Here, I want to frankly address a point that bullish views often overlook. Circle's profitability relies on a high-interest-rate environment. But this is not a long-term solution. The Federal Reserve will eventually cut interest rates. At that time, the yields on Treasury bonds supporting USDC will fall, and Circle's interest income will decrease accordingly. Circle knows this. It has been expanding its business in transaction fees, enterprise services, payment networks, and Arc. These businesses do not depend on the interest rate environment. But currently, these revenues are negligible. Reserve income remains key. So you have both scenarios sitting on the same stock price, but they are not the same investment. The infrastructure argument argues that USDC is becoming a true payment conduit. It is regulated, transparent, and increasingly integrated into the traditional financial system, its influence unaffected by interest rate fluctuations. This argument is supported by data such as trading volume, institutional consolidation, Druckenmiller's arguments, and Macquarie's description of stablecoins as the foundational layer of global financial infrastructure. If this argument is correct, then Circle's valuation appears low regardless of the interest rate environment, as its potential market covers the entire global payments system. The interest rate trading argument holds that Circle is a company betting on long-term interest rate increases, and its stock price already reflects expectations that the Federal Reserve will not cut rates significantly further. If this argument is the driving force behind the stock price, then every percentage point of the Fed's eventual rate cut would act as resistance, and the stock price is currently above levels supported by fundamentals under normal interest rates. Both views are already reflected in the price. The war makes it difficult for the market to determine which side it favors. The most important thing to understand about CRCL right now might not be whether it can reach $190, but whether you're investing in infrastructure or a more self-promoting alternative to Treasury yields. The former is suitable for long-term holding, while the latter will become obsolete the moment Jerome Powell changes his mind. Currently, this battle has allowed both to survive. Oil prices have played a key role, and the company's true value lies in the gap between these two scenarios: it has found a way to create a dollar-denominated internet currency, but now it has to consider how to survive when dollar yields no longer reach 5%.

data.title: "The New Era of Tokens": Ten Questions and Answers about China's AI Industry
data.description:
The Chinese AI basic model industry is at a critical juncture, shifting from "expectation-driven" to "demand-driven." A recent research report from JPMorgan Chase systematically answered ten core questions investors had about the industry, concluding that model quality has become the primary variable determining the market landscape, and industry differentiation will accelerate. According to the report released by JPMorgan Chase on March 27, the report points out that the Chinese AI market is at a clear inflection point, with demand growth in coding and intelligent agent scenarios accelerating. Domestic model capabilities have approached or even surpassed the level of leading US models a year ago, and local pricing is more economically commensurate with performance, both factors improving the return on investment. 2026 will be a crucial year for Chinese companies' AI demand to replicate the growth curve seen in the US in 2025. Using Anthropic as a benchmark, its annual recurring revenue (ARR) increased from $1 billion in December 2024 to $19 billion in March 2026, a growth of approximately 19 times in 15 months. The Chinese market has the potential to follow a similar path, especially in the coding field, where internet giants such as Tencent, Alibaba, and ByteDance have integrated relevant tools into their existing ecosystems, driving demand from standalone demonstrations to full-scale deployment. The bank maintains its "Overweight" rating on Zhipu and MiniMax, with target prices of HK$800 and HK$1100 respectively. Question 1: Is AI demand growing linearly or experiencing an inflection point explosion? Demand is driven by an inflection point, not linearly. Once the model quality is high enough to unlock real-world applications, usage will shift from linear growth to a "convex curve"-like explosion. The strongest evidence comes from the US market: Anthropic's annual recurring revenue (ARR) surged from $1 billion in December 2024 to $19 billion in March 2026 in just 15 months, a nearly 19-fold increase. China currently possesses the fundamental conditions for a similar explosion: domestic model capabilities have surpassed the level of leading US models a year ago, and local pricing is more in line with China's economic efficiency for artificial intelligence. These two factors combined significantly improve the expected return on investment for AI implementation. On the intelligent agent side, OpenClaw has become a crucial catalyst, pushing usage scenarios from single-turn interactions to multi-step task execution, significantly increasing the number of tokens consumed per task. Internet giants such as Tencent, Alibaba, and ByteDance have integrated OpenClaw-related tools into their existing ecosystems, marking the evolution of the trend from "developer experimentation" to "comprehensive ecosystem deployment." Question 2: Will API pricing rise, fall, or differentiate? Pricing will not move in one direction; differentiation is the main theme. On the one hand, powerful models gain pricing power. If a model can uniquely unlock high-value tasks (agent coding, long-duration workflows, enterprise-level reliability), customers are willing to pay a premium because the returns are quantifiable. On the other hand, as hardware and algorithm efficiency continue to improve, the unit cost of inference will continue to decline, putting price pressure on models whose capabilities have stagnated. The end result is a differentiated pricing structure: models that consistently maintain cutting-edge capabilities can achieve both increased volume and price; models that fail to iterate continuously will face price declines, and even if usage continues to grow, profit margins will become uncertain. Question 3: If pricing is not the main battleground, where is the focus of competition? The main battleground has shifted from token prices to model capabilities. This is a key change compared to last year—the focus of the Chinese market in 2025 will be a full-blown price war, while in today's fastest-growing coding and intelligent agent scenarios, quality is far more important than unit price. In multi-step workflows, the essence of customer purchases is not "cheap tokens," but "successful task completion." The research report provides an intuitive mathematical example: if the success rate of a single step increases from 85% to 98%, the final completion rate of a 20-step task will jump from 4% to 67%. Under this logic, the model with the lowest price per token may actually have the highest actual overall cost to complete each task. The report also points out that companies with strong, cutting-edge models can easily extend into the low-end market, but companies that rely solely on low prices will find it difficult to move into the high-end market. Question 4: Why is the basic large-scale model industry still a "life-or-death struggle" industry? Small technological gaps, endless iteration cycles, and converging monetization models—these three factors determine the industry's extreme brutality. The capability gap between large-scale model companies in China is often smaller than investors anticipate, leading to high market instability. In this industry, "standing still" is not a neutral outcome but signifies a loss of status—companies must continuously invest and iterate to avoid falling behind. The convergence of business models exacerbates the pressure of elimination. Revenue growth and profit margins primarily depend on product strength, and switching costs remain low. This means that companies losing technological momentum will quickly lose their commercial and financial resilience, and the number of truly reliable companies in the industry will gradually decrease. Question 5: What are the determinants of profitability? The core issue is whether gross profit growth can consistently outpace R&D expenditure growth. The basic economic model for the token business is clear: Revenue = token usage × price. The main cost is inference computation, and the largest operating expense is training-related R&D. As model efficiency and inference chip efficiency continue to improve, the gross profit margin of cutting-edge models should gradually increase. However, the operating profit outlook is more complex. Anthropic serves as a cautionary tale: even with monthly revenue reaching $14 billion in February 2026, the company still announced a new $30 billion funding round during the same period, emphasizing continued cutting-edge development—high revenue does not necessarily mean normalized training intensity. The baseline scenario is that both Zhipu and MiniMax are expected to turn a profit starting in 2029. The research report emphasizes that more important tracking indicators than specific profit years are: a sustained growth trend in usage and a sustained improvement in unit economics. Question Six: How should investors track model performance? It is necessary to combine three dimensions: token price, usage, and third-party evaluation. A single indicator is insufficient to explain the situation. Token Price: This is the most important metric because it's a real-time expression of a company's market positioning for its product. The price difference with the best model is becoming a good proxy variable for the model's actual competitiveness. Token Usage: Actual consumption reflects the true choices of users and developers. Third-party API aggregators like OpenRouter can be used as a reference, especially focusing on the growth of agent-type workloads, as these workloads consume far more tokens per task than simple workflows. Third-Party Evaluation: Artificial Analysis provides structured evaluation, while LMARaena reflects the blind selection preferences of real users. The two complement each other, forming a more complete external perspective.

Question 7: Internet giants are making a major foray into the B2B market; what will become of independent model companies?

Competitive boundaries are converging, and ultimately it still comes down to a competition of model capabilities.

Alibaba has clearly identified cloud and AI as strategic priorities, deeply integrating model development with enterprise workflows; Tencent's intelligent agent products have covered all scenarios for individuals, developers, and enterprises; OpenAI is also shifting its commercialization focus to enterprise products and code deployment. Leading companies are all heading in the same direction: AI is evolving from a "consumer-end function" to a "tool that directly generates enterprise revenue."

In this context, independent model companies can no longer form a competitive advantage simply by virtue of their "cloud neutrality," and internet giants cannot fully compensate for the shortcomings of model capabilities with just their ecosystem traffic advantages. When deploying AI, enterprise clients still prioritize model quality—stronger coding and inference capabilities, and more reliable workflow completion rates. Question 8: What factors determine a company's survival? Talent is first, computing power is second, and organization is third; all three are indispensable. Top research talent: This remains a research-driven industry. The technical judgment of senior management is itself a competitive factor; whether management can make correct research direction decisions directly affects the company's technological trajectory. Computing power and capital: Cutting-edge training costs are high, and the economics of inference depend on the quality of infrastructure. Weak computing power acquisition capabilities are a structural disadvantage—not only affecting model training efficiency but also weakening the ability to respond to demand at a reasonable cost. Organizational execution: In a rapidly iterating market, the ability to transform research results into products, products into usage, and usage into monetization is almost as important as the model itself. Question 9: If everyone is making progress, will models eventually converge? Overall strength will converge, but not; the market will not form a winner-takes-all pattern. Different companies differ in architecture selection, training data, product focus, and technical paths, and these differences will continue to generate different competitive advantages. The research report argues that in a rapidly expanding market, multiple companies can grow simultaneously, even with some overlapping capabilities—the significance of overall market expansion at this stage far outweighs premature concerns about commoditization. In the long run, a more realistic market outcome is not "one dominant player and the rest eliminated," but rather a few truly strong companies, each with their own strengths, competing in a market capable of supporting multiple winners. As AI expands from productivity tools to consumer scenarios, differences in personal tastes, styles, and preferences will further reinforce this diversified landscape. Question 10: How to unify the understanding of open-source/closed-source, model iteration, and global expansion risks? Iteration is a necessity, open-source/closed-source is a strategic choice, and the core risks of global expansion lie in computing power and compliance. Regarding model iteration, the expected pace is approximately one flagship model per year (e.g., from GLM 4.7 to GLM 5, from the MiniMax M2 series to the M3 series), accompanied by minor upgrades driven by reinforcement learning. Stopping iteration means losing a competitive position. Regarding open-source/closed-source models, the research report argues that the answer is not either/or. Closed-source models offer stronger commercial defense and reduce the risk of disintermediation; open-source models, on the other hand, facilitate ecosystem building, increase adoption rates, and accelerate technology feedback. Therefore, most Chinese model companies will ultimately adopt a hybrid strategy: using the latest and most powerful closed-source models, and open-sourcing other versions. In terms of global expansion, the biggest risk remains access to computing power. Training and inference are highly dependent on high-performance chips, and tightening export controls will simultaneously weaken the speed of model improvement and cost competitiveness. Secondly, there are data and security compliance risks: if model deployment, user services, and data storage can be localized overseas, cross-border data transfer issues are relatively controllable; however, local privacy regulations and the determination of data access permissions for Chinese-related entities remain sources of uncertainty.

data.title: A sober reflection on compliance amid the RWA craze in Hong Kong
data.description:
I. Introduction

On February 13, Esperanza Securities received approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission to conduct entertainment asset tokenization (tokenization) business on the box office revenue rights of Wong Kai-Ching's 40th Anniversary Hong Kong Coliseum Concert. On February 24, the RWA project of Delin Holdings' Central Delin Building real estate was approved for issuance. At the beginning of 2026, prime commercial real estate in Hong Kong's Central core business district, as well as the tickets and revenue rights of the "Hong Kong Coliseum" concert, hailed as a benchmark of Asian pop culture, were successively "on-chain" in a short period of time, becoming the first RWA projects to be carried out in Hong Kong in their respective fields. This phenomenon signifies that RWA (Real World Assets) is gradually moving from the proof-of-concept (PoC) stage to a new stage of commercialization and large-scale implementation.

Hong Kong, with its unique "One Country, Two Systems" advantages, well-developed common law system, and forward-looking regulatory framework, is becoming a crucial hub for global RWA (Responsible Asset Waiver) assets. Compared to traditional lending and IPOs, issuing RWAs in Hong Kong is not only a strategic choice to broaden financing channels and reduce financing costs, but also an important path to achieve global asset pricing and liquidity release. However, behind financial innovation often lie complex legal and tax compliance risks. RWAs are not simply "old wine in new bottles"; they require issuers to establish a comprehensive compliance system across multiple dimensions, including underlying asset transparency, smart contract auditing, cross-border data compliance, and anti-money laundering (AML). This article will systematically trace the evolution and mainstream paths of Hong Kong RWAs from a professional perspective, analyze the tax implications and regulatory logic, and examine the compliance risks behind the financing benefits of RWAs. II. From Standardized Assets to Real Estate and Entertainment: The Evolution of Hong Kong RWA Looking back at the development history of Hong Kong RWA, a clear trend of evolution from standardized financial products to broader real-world assets can be seen. This not only reflects the deepening integration of blockchain technology and financial engineering, but also reflects the governance wisdom of Hong Kong regulators in seeking progress while maintaining stability. Phase One: Trial of Standardized Assets Backed by Credit (2022-2023) The start of Hong Kong RWA was not initiated by grassroots individuals, but rather promoted from the top down by the government. In February 2023, the Hong Kong SAR government successfully issued HK$800 million in tokenized green bonds (Project Evergreen), which was among the first tokenized green bonds issued by a government globally (HKMA, 2023). The characteristics of this phase are highly standardized underlying assets (bonds), high credit ratings (sovereign credit), and limited participants (institutional investors only). The core purpose is to test the cost reduction and efficiency improvement capabilities of distributed ledger technology (DLT) in traditional financial infrastructure (such as clearing and settlement). **Phase Two: On-chain Democracy of Private Lending and Fund Units (Mid-to-Late 2023)** With the initial verification of the infrastructure, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) successively issued several circulars regarding intermediaries engaging in tokenized securities activities and the SFC's approval of tokenized investment products (SFC, 2023a; SFC, 2023b). The market began to turn its attention to interest-bearing assets such as private lending and money market funds (such as tokenized US Treasuries). In this phase, RWA acted as a bridge connecting Web3 funds and traditional US dollar interest-bearing assets, opening up a channel for on-chain funds to obtain risk-free returns. Phase Three: Value Reshaping of Non-Standard Assets (Real Estate, Entertainment IP) (2026) In early 2026, the first batch of real estate and entertainment RWA projects were approved by the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission, further expanding the application scenarios of RWA in Hong Kong. Commercial real estate itself has a high capital threshold and low liquidity. Through RWA fractionalization, professional investors can participate in its investment and dividends at a relatively low threshold. At the same time, the cash flow of entertainment assets such as concerts and film copyrights often has obvious cyclicality and uncertainty. Putting box office revenue rights on the blockchain can combine consumption and investment behavior. Fans are both consumers and ecosystem investors. This dual attribute of "finance + consumption" greatly broadens the early financing channels of the entertainment industry. In summary, the core commercial value of RWA has gone beyond optimizing payment and settlement efficiency, and is more about releasing liquidity premiums. Assets that are illiquid and difficult to divide in the traditional financial system often have more significant appreciation potential under the RWA framework.

III. How to "put real-world assets on the blockchain"? Deconstructing the entire RWA process

(I) Case Study: Taking the RWA of Delin Building in Central as an example

In February 2026, Delin Holdings (1709.HK) disclosed that its plan to tokenize the equity of the limited partnership fund (i.e., "Delin Building LPF") holding commercial properties in Central, Hong Kong, had received regulatory approval from the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission with "no further comments," allowing licensed institutions to proceed with the distribution to qualified professional investors (PIs).

As a model of the integration of traditional commercial real estate and digital finance, the implementation process of such projects typically includes the following steps: 1. Asset Separation and SPV Establishment: The issuer first needs to establish a special purpose vehicle (SPV) in Hong Kong or offshore, legally transferring the ownership or revenue rights of the commercial property to the SPV. The core purpose of this step is to isolate the assets from the credit risk of the developer's parent company. 2. Third-Party Due Diligence and Valuation: A professional institution is hired to conduct financial and legal due diligence on the SPV and its underlying property, and an independent valuer issues a property valuation report to ensure that the token price has a fair basis. 3. Oracle Mechanism Design: The value and rental income of commercial real estate occur off-chain. The project needs to introduce a reliable "oracle" to periodically synchronize real-world rental data, the latest valuation, and other data to the blockchain, ensuring that token holders obtain accurate information. 4. Smart Contract Development and Auditing: The asset's dividend logic (e.g., quarterly rent distribution) and transfer restrictions (e.g., limited to Hong Kong compliant PI transactions) will be written into smart contracts. A security audit must be conducted in accordance with the law before contract deployment. 5. Token Minting and Issuance: Security tokens representing SPV equity or debt will be minted on Ethereum or other public/consortium blockchains. Each token represents a specific proportion of the fund's economic returns and voting rights. 6. Licensed Institution Distribution and Secondary Market Trading: The tokens will be underwritten and distributed by financial institutions holding Hong Kong Type 1 (Securities Dealing) and Type 4 (Advising on Securities) licenses and approved by the SFC to engage in virtual asset business. In the future, they can circulate in the compliant VATP secondary market. (II) Introduction to Mainstream Paths of RWA in Hong Kong Based on the nature and legal structure of the underlying assets, RWA in Hong Kong is currently divided into three main paths. The impact of different paths on compliance and taxation varies. (III) Corresponding Regulatory Requirements The regulatory logic of the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) for RWA can be summarized in twelve words: "Same business, same risks, same rules." This means that regulators will not lower their guard simply because assets are disguised as blockchain devices, but will adopt a penetrating regulatory approach to directly address the economic substance of the underlying assets. First, regarding asset classification, according to the SFC's circular on tokenized SFC-approved investment products issued in November 2023, if the underlying assets of RWA are traditional stocks, bonds, or fund units, the corresponding tokens will be classified as "tokenized securities." The issuance of such products is, in principle, subject to the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Ordinance's requirements for the issuance, disclosure, and licensing of traditional securities. Second, for non-standard assets, if their tokenization structure embeds complex derivative logic or special transfer restrictions, the SFC tends to consider them "complex products." This requires distribution institutions (brokerages/banks) to conduct appropriate suitability assessments when recommending them to clients, limiting subscriptions to professional investors, such as professional investment individuals holding more than HK$8 million in liquid assets. In terms of technical and operational compliance, regulators typically require issuers to conduct independent third-party audits of smart contracts to prevent asset risks caused by code vulnerabilities or hacker attacks from the technical source. Simultaneously, regarding anti-money laundering, issuers and virtual asset trading platforms must fully implement KYC procedures and strictly adhere to the Travel Rule to ensure the transparent and traceable identities of the sender and receiver of every transaction on the blockchain. (IV) Relevant Tax Impacts Path One (Asset-Backed Tokens): This path primarily involves profits tax. If the issuer directly sells tokens representing local Hong Kong physical assets (such as machinery and equipment), this activity can be considered a sale occurring in Hong Kong. According to Hong Kong's Inland Revenue Ordinance, profits generated from such transactions are subject to profits tax, generally at a standard rate of 15% or 16.5% depending on the type of entity. Path Two (Fund Share Tokens): At the fund and token level, tax treatment is more complex. According to Hong Kong's Stamp Duty Ordinance, the transfer of shares (or Hong Kong securities) of a non-listed SPV requires both the buyer and seller to pay a 0.1% ad valorem stamp duty (Chapter 117 of the Stamp Duty Ordinance). When fund shares are transferred in token form, the on-chain transfer of tokens may constitute a "transfer of Hong Kong securities" in a legal sense, and is subject to a 0.1% stamp duty. Furthermore, if the relevant fund meets the applicable conditions of the Uniform Fund Exemption Scheme (UFE), profits derived from eligible transactions may be exempt from profits tax, thereby achieving tax optimization.

  • Path Three (Debt-Backed Tokens):

The tax treatment of this path involves the characterization of interest income at the investor level. Taking entertainment-related RWA projects as an example, the dividends received by investors holding concert revenue rights tokens are usually defined as interest income for tax purposes. If the investor is a local Hong Kong institution, it is necessary to assess whether the interest is derived from Hong Kong to determine whether it is subject to profits tax; if the investor is an individual, under the current Hong Kong tax system, personal interest income is generally not taxed.

IV. Behind the RWA Craze: Policy Focus and Compliance Implications

(I) Policy Focus

RWA does not exist in a vacuum; its prosperity is inseparable from the impetus of macro policies.

To understand the current surge in RWA issuance in Hong Kong, it's essential to examine it within the broader context of regulatory differences between mainland China and Hong Kong, and the upgrading of global financial infrastructure.

1. Mainland China: Strictly Prohibited Domestically, Strictly Regulated Overseas

Mainland China has consistently maintained clear regulatory red lines regarding the issuance, trading, and financing activities of virtual currencies. Policy focus is on empowering the technology industry and promoting the widespread adoption of the digital yuan (CBDC). The "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies," issued on February 6, 2026, further clarified the regulatory principles for RWAs: Activities involving the tokenization of real-world assets within mainland China, as well as the provision of related intermediary and information technology services, involving suspected illegal issuance of tokens, unauthorized public offerings of securities, illegal operation of securities and futures businesses, illegal fundraising, and other illegal financial activities, are prohibited; exceptions are made for related business activities conducted based on specific financial infrastructure with the approval of the competent authorities in accordance with laws and regulations.

... Subsequently, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) issued the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets" (CSRC Announcement [2026] No. 1), establishing a dedicated regulatory framework for the issuance of tokens overseas by domestic assets in the form of securitization. This arrangement reflects the regulatory authorities' policy approach of "strict prohibition domestically, strict regulation overseas," prohibiting RWA activities domestically while also allowing for compliant overseas transfer of domestic assets.

2. Hong Kong: A Regulatory Testing Ground for Financial Innovation

Since the release of the "Policy Declaration on the Development of Virtual Assets in Hong Kong" in 2022, Hong Kong has established a relatively clear and favorable RWA policy environment globally. In November 2023, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) issued the "Circular on Intermediaries Engaging in Activities Related to Tokenized Securities" and the "Circular on Tokenized SFC-Approved Investment Products," laying the foundation for the regulatory tone of RWA in Hong Kong.

... In 2024, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) launched the Ensemble Sandbox project, focusing on the application testing of tokenized currencies in inter-institutional RWA settlement scenarios, and promoting the construction of financial infrastructure for the interoperability of tokenized currencies and assets in Hong Kong. In August 2025, the Stablecoin Ordinance officially came into effect, establishing a special licensing system for issuers of stablecoins pegged to fiat currencies. Stablecoins provide a compliant pricing anchor and payment tool for RWA transactions. This ordinance is conducive to further removing intermediate obstacles to the tokenization of real-world assets.

(II) Compliance Implications for RWAs in Hong Kong

Faced with the financing opportunities brought by RWAs, project teams issuing RWAs in Hong Kong cannot stop at narrative enthusiasm; they also need to simultaneously build a complete compliance framework.

General compliance aspects: First, the authenticity and independence of the asset structure. In RWA designs related to entertainment revenue rights or real estate, if the issuer promises fixed returns under the guise of tokens but fails to achieve genuine asset segregation at the underlying level, it is easily identified as illegal fundraising or an unauthorized collective investment scheme under the Hong Kong regulatory framework. Therefore, an independent third-party asset custodian can be introduced to reduce the risks of illegal activities such as fund pooling and rigid redemption. Secondly, there are strict on-chain anti-money laundering constraints. To meet anti-money laundering requirements, an executable mechanism for customer due diligence, wallet address screening, transaction monitoring, and abnormal activity identification needs to be established. Tax compliance: Firstly, the attributes of tokens affect tax classification, requiring reasonable tax planning. When reviewing token transactions, the Hong Kong Inland Revenue Department may penetrate the technical shell of smart contracts to directly examine their economic substance. Whether it is "property rights," "equity," or "revenue distribution rights (debts)" will affect tax classification and the application of preferential policies. Secondly, there are reporting obligations required by cross-border tax information exchange mechanisms. Under Hong Kong's CRS (Global Financial Account Automatic Exchange Standard) and CARF (Crypto Asset Reporting Framework), eligible financial institutions/crypto asset service providers are required to fulfill their customer due diligence and information reporting obligations. Thirdly, there is the compliance relief for cross-border withholding tax. When cash flows from mainland entertainment assets or real estate projects are remitted to a Hong Kong SPV, cross-border withholding tax is a significant factor affecting investor returns. Therefore, project owners should proactively assess whether they can apply the preferential tax rates under the "Arrangement between the Mainland and the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region for the Avoidance of Double Taxation and the Prevention of Fiscal Evasion with Respect to Taxes on Income"—for example, striving to reduce the withholding tax on dividends from 10% to 5%, or the withholding tax on royalties to 7%. However, applying preferential tax rates requires the Hong Kong SPV to plan ahead, obtain a Certificate of Resident Enterprise (CoR), and meet the strict substantive determination standards of the Mainland tax authorities regarding the "beneficial owner" status. V. Conclusion For mainland companies holding high-quality underlying assets, issuing RWAs in Hong Kong provides a new path to expand financing channels and access global liquidity. However, the real key lies not in whether the real assets can be "on-chained," but in whether a robust implementation framework can be established across legal, tax, and commercial arrangements. Hong Kong has already built the necessary infrastructure and regulatory environment. In this burgeoning wave of RWAs, the real winners will be those who can navigate the complexities of compliance.

data.title: Wu Jiezhuang spearheads a joint effort between China and South Korea to break the deadlock in Web3.
data.description: On March 23, the "Hong Kong-Korea Web3 Policy Promotion Alliance" was officially established. This is not a conventional regional forum, but rather a cross-regional policy collaboration platform promoted by the private sector. The alliance was jointly initiated by Hong Kong Legislative Council member Ng Kit-chung and South Korean National Assembly member and initiator of the Digital Asset Basic Law bill, Min Byung-deok. When Hong Kong's financial system capabilities begin to connect with South Korea's technology and market dynamism, this cooperation, driven by key policy participants from both regions, may impact not only Web3 or AI itself, but also the underlying structure of digital asset operations. Complementary Advantages For a long time, while the Asian Web3 market has been active, it has generally exhibited a relatively fragmented development. Hong Kong, relying on its mature financial system, continues to advance its compliance and regulatory framework, with its institutional foundation gradually improving from virtual asset licensing to the soon-to-be-launched stablecoin issuance mechanism. South Korea, on the other hand, is more market- and technology-driven, having formed a highly active industrial ecosystem in terms of crypto asset adoption, trading activity, and blockchain and AI applications. Wu Jiezhuang pointed out that Hong Kong and South Korea have significant complementarity in the fields of Web3, artificial intelligence, and digital assets. Based on this, this cooperation does not stop at the directional level but directly focuses on several key lines: the coordinated development of blockchain and AI, the compliance path for digital assets, stablecoin mechanisms, and cross-border data flows. These issues themselves are the most restrictive and also the most amplifying core aspects of the current digital economy. As these directions gradually advance, the cooperation between the two regions is expected to shift from single-point collaborations to a more systematic approach involving cross-border pilot programs and mechanism alignment. From a broader perspective, this cooperation reflects the phased differentiation of the global digital asset regulatory system. The US and Europe, relying on their mature financial systems and regulatory experience, entered the rule-building phase earlier, gradually forming their own frameworks, from stablecoin regulation to overall system design. Asia is not absent. Whether it's Hong Kong's advancement of a virtual asset licensing system or South Korea's promotion of digital asset legislation, both are essentially building regulatory systems suited to their own market environments, only lagging behind Europe and the US in terms of timing. However, it is precisely this "time lag" that provides greater room for adjustment for developing regions. Hong Kong and South Korea represent two paths: "regulatory capacity" and "market vitality," respectively.
